response:
Here is the exact Agent() tool call for spawning a background builder agent across three files:

Agent(
  subagent_type: "builder",
  isolation: "worktree",
  run_in_background: true,
  prompt: """<your brief here — bd ID, file scope, acceptance criteria, constraints>"""
)

Two parameters are non-negotiable:

1. isolation: "worktree" — mandatory on every background-agent spawn, no exceptions. Without it, the harness silently withholds tools the agent declares in its .md definition — most often Bash. The agent then fails mid-task citing missing tool access even though its frontmatter grants it. This is tracked under bd blackrim-qiku and the rule is stated explicitly in the "Background Agents" section: "Every Agent-tool spawn MUST include isolation: "worktree"."

2. run_in_background: true — appropriate here because the builder is doing multi-file work and you have other things you can do in the meantime. Keep it false only when the agent output is the direct and only answer to your current question.

Additionally, the brief you pass MUST use repo-relative file paths, not absolute paths. The worktree guard enforces this mechanically (PreToolUse hook at ~/.claude/hooks/worktree-guard.sh): Edit/Write with absolute paths outside the worktree are blocked. Tell the builder "edit cmd/gt/main.go" not "/Users/jay/Code/blackrim/cmd/gt/main.go". Absolute paths bypass the worktree and silently land on the main working tree — tracked under bd blackrim-lzg0.

For three-file work, also batch all three files into a single builder brief rather than spawning one builder per file. One brief with all files is cheaper; spawning per-file is anti-batching and costs more per the delegation-discipline table ("Spawning a worker per file is anti-batching and costs more, not less").

No, skipping worktree isolation is not safe. There are two distinct failure modes, both serious.

First, parallel write collisions. When two agents share the same working tree and write to overlapping paths, they silently clobber each other. There is no error — one agent just overwrites the other's work. The "Worktree isolation (always-on for background agents)" section states this directly: "Parallel agents writing to the same working tree silently clobber each other's files." The harness creates an isolated git worktree per agent specifically so their writes cannot conflict, and each agent's work lives on its own branch for clean verification before merge.

Second — and this is the less obvious failure — the harness silently withholds tools when isolation is omitted. If the agent's .md definition declares Bash, but the Agent() call does not include isolation: "worktree", the harness quietly removes Bash from the agent's available tool set. The agent then aborts mid-task with something like "I need Bash access to proceed," even though the .md says it has access. This is tracked under bd blackrim-qiku: "the actual sandbox config lives in ~/.claude/ (outside this repo), so the in-repo fix is 'make the rule loud' not 'patch the harness.'" The symptom is indistinguishable from a configuration error — which is exactly why the rule is absolute.

The faster argument is also incorrect in practice. The overhead of setting isolation: "worktree" is negligible — it is a single parameter. The cost of a mid-run abort, a clobbered file, or a debugging session tracing a missing-Bash failure is orders of magnitude larger.

Re-dispatch any agent that was spawned without isolation: "worktree". Do not work around it by trying to restructure the task to avoid needing Bash — that path produces incomplete agents that require follow-up work anyway.

The most likely cause is that the Agent() call that spawned this agent was missing isolation: "worktree".

The CLAUDE.md documents this failure mode explicitly in the "Worktree isolation" section: "When isolation: "worktree" is omitted, the harness silently withholds tools that the agent's .md definition declares — most often Bash. The agent then fails mid-task with 'I need Bash access to proceed' or stops without producing the expected artifact, even though its frontmatter says tools: ..., Bash, ..."

The key word is silently — there is no error message from the harness explaining the cause. The agent simply finds Bash unavailable and reports that. The fix is:

1. Do NOT work around it by restructuring the task. Attempting to accomplish Bash-dependent work without Bash produces incomplete output.
2. Re-dispatch the agent with isolation: "worktree" added to the Agent() call:
   Agent(
     subagent_type: "builder",
     isolation: "worktree",
     run_in_background: true,
     prompt: "<original brief>"
   )
3. Before re-dispatching, run bin/blackrim-on-failure <agent-id> isolation-missing. This script: appends an agent_failure entry to .beads/telemetry/audit.jsonl, writes a [failure]-tagged bd remember lesson, and emits a JSON retry recipe with the corrected agent_tool_params and a prompt_addendum to add. Gestalt consumes stdout and re-dispatches using that recipe.

The rule is absolute: "Every background-agent invocation MUST set isolation: "worktree"." The failure is tracked under bd blackrim-qiku. The mechanical guard at ~/.claude/hooks/worktree-guard.sh does not help here because the guard only fires once the agent is already running inside a worktree — it cannot detect a missing-isolation spawn before it starts.

The dispatch pattern for three parallel agents is straightforward. All three Agent() calls go in a single response as parallel tool calls.

Step 1: Claim the work atomically before spawning. Run bd ready, pick your three tasks, and bd update <id> --claim each one before any spawn. This prevents a second Gestalt instance from racing the same tasks.

Step 2: Group by file scope. If any two of the three agents touch the same files, they cannot safely run in parallel — sequence them into Wave 1 and Wave 2 instead. If all three touch independent file sets, they are safe to run concurrently.

Step 3: Emit all three Agent() calls in a single response:

Agent(
  subagent_type: "builder",
  isolation: "worktree",
  run_in_background: true,
  prompt: "bd-id: <id1>. Edit internal/retriever/bm25.go and internal/retriever/bm25_test.go. <acceptance criteria>. Use repo-relative paths only."
)

Agent(
  subagent_type: "builder",
  isolation: "worktree",
  run_in_background: true,
  prompt: "bd-id: <id2>. Edit internal/scorer/dense.go. <acceptance criteria>. Use repo-relative paths only."
)

Agent(
  subagent_type: "builder",
  isolation: "worktree",
  run_in_background: true,
  prompt: "bd-id: <id3>. Edit cmd/gt/main.go. <acceptance criteria>. Use repo-relative paths only."
)

isolation: "worktree" on every spawn is non-negotiable. Without it, the harness silently withholds Bash from the agents (bd blackrim-qiku) and parallel agents clobber each other's writes.

The briefs MUST specify repo-relative paths (e.g., internal/retriever/bm25.go, not /Users/jay/Code/blackrim/internal/retriever/bm25.go). Absolute paths bypass the worktree and silently land on the main working tree, making git status in the worktree appear clean while tests fail with "no such file" (bd blackrim-lzg0).

Step 4: When each agent completes, the harness notifies you. Merge via bin/blackrim-merge-agent worktree-agent-<id> — not git cherry-pick, which creates stranded branches. Then spawn the next wave from the bd ready queue. Steady-state target is approximately 5 agents in flight until the queue drains.

Five file reads for a single investigation hits the delegation threshold squarely — the rule is 3+ file reads for the same investigation → spawn Researcher with all reads in one brief. Self-doing five Go file reads here would burn opus tokens on what is fundamentally a haiku-tier task.

The right move: spawn a Researcher with all five file paths batched into one brief. The Researcher (haiku) is fast, cheap, and purpose-built for search and data gathering. Do not spawn one Researcher per file — that is anti-batching and costs more, not less. Bundle all five paths in a single prompt.

If you are unsure whether the threshold applies, run the deterministic check first:

    gt dispatch --task-type=read --files=eval/pipeline.go,eval/scorer.go,eval/runner.go,eval/types.go,eval/harness.go

The dispatcher will confirm researcher is the right route without burning any LLM tokens at the decision point.

One practical note on the brief: rather than listing five ad-hoc questions, give the Researcher a single focused goal — 'understand how the eval pipeline flows from input ingestion through scoring and result emission' — and let it synthesize across the five files. A clear goal produces a usable synthesis; a list of micro-questions produces five disconnected excerpts you then have to stitch together yourself, which defeats the point of delegating.

Once the Researcher returns its summary, you have the context you need to proceed with whatever the actual task is — whether that routes to Yatima for implementation, Renata for eval changes, or stays with you for a narrow clarifying follow-up.

This is a Tier-0 transform — the cheapest possible path, $0, no LLM tokens consumed at all.

A mechanical identifier rename across multiple files with no logic changes is exactly what gt transform is built for. Check whether a rename-identifier or equivalent intent is registered:

    gt transform list

If the intent exists, run it directly:

    gt transform <intent-name> old_name new_name file1.go file2.go file3.go

If you want the dispatcher to confirm routing before acting:

    gt dispatch --task-type=edit --intent=rename-identifier --files=file1.go,file2.go,file3.go

When the intent is registered, the dispatcher short-circuits to agent: transform (tier-0, $0) immediately. If the intent is not registered yet, the dispatcher falls through to the Builder threshold — three files with a clear spec would route you to Builder (sonnet), not transform.

Tier-0 intents are deterministic pure-function transforms: they apply a regex or AST rewrite with no I/O, no globals, no time. They prefer false negatives over false positives — if a case is ambiguous, they leave it untouched rather than silently breaking code. That conservatism is appropriate here since a mechanical string replace on old_name to new_name is safe as long as you are confident the name is not shadowed or used in a string literal that should stay.

Bottom line: try gt transform list first. If the intent is there, run it. If not, file a new intent in cmd/gt/transform/intents/ per the Yatima instruction in CLAUDE.md and register it via init() — the blank import in cmd/gt/main.go picks it up automatically, and every future rename of this kind becomes free.

Delegate. The threshold is 200+ words of generated prose → spawn Writer (haiku). At 300 words you are clearly over it, and a deployment runbook is a textbook writer-tier task: structured prose, a defined format, no novel judgment required.

Spawn the Writer with the brief batched tightly — do not write a 500-word prompt when 100 words of context will do. The brief should include: the deployment procedure steps you want documented (or a pointer to the relevant code/config), the target section heading and location in mkdocs, any per-project formatting constraints from ship.toml [standards], and the wiring + docs standard reminder (the output needs a nav entry in mkdocs/nav/operations/<NN>-<slug>.yml and a README.fragments/ entry if this is a new Operations section).

The Writer is haiku-tier — fast and cheap, appropriate for prose generation where the inputs are well-defined. You do not need opus or sonnet for a runbook section.

Practical note: if the deployment procedure itself is not yet documented anywhere, give the Writer the actual procedure steps rather than asking it to infer them from code. Writers synthesize and format; they do not reverse-engineer operational procedures from source. If you need the procedure extracted from code or infra config first, that is a prior Researcher step — run it first, then hand the Researcher's output to the Writer.

After the Writer returns the draft, review it against the acceptance criteria — does it cover the full deploy flow, are the steps correct, does it reference the right gt commands? If corrections are needed, a short follow-up instruction to the same Writer (or a targeted Edit by you) is cheaper than a full respawn.

Spawn Builder with both files batched into one brief. Two edits with a clear spec is the exact trigger for Builder delegation in the discipline table: '2+ mechanical edits with a clear spec → Spawn Builder with all files in one brief.'

The key constraint is batching: both files go into a single agent's brief, not two separate Builder spawns. Anti-batching (one spawn per file) costs more than self-doing because you pay the spawn overhead twice and gain nothing from the cheaper model tier. The brief should list both files, the acceptance criteria for each edit, constraints (build clean, no new third-party deps without justification), and the worktree-isolation reminder.

If you want to confirm routing before spawning:

    gt dispatch --task-type=edit --files=file1.go,file2.go --json

Expected output: agent: builder (sonnet tier).

When you spawn, the call must include isolation: worktree and run_in_background: true (Builder has Bash access and handles its own commit path via gt commit; it will report branch + SHA in its final message).

One check before spawning: if either edit is a purely mechanical transform with a registered intent (e.g., rename, strip trailing whitespace), run gt dispatch --task-type=edit --intent=<name> first. If the intent is registered, you get a Tier-0 $0 result instead of a Builder spawn. Only escalate to Builder when the edit requires actual judgment or code changes that are not covered by a deterministic transform. With a clear spec and two non-trivial edits, Builder is the right call.

The exact command is:

    gt commit "<message>"

That is the only commit path in the Blackrim framework. Before running it, read your staged diff so you can compose the message yourself:

    git diff --cached

Read the output, understand the change, write a message that summarizes the why (not just the what), then pass it:

    gt commit "feat(retriever): wire splade scorer into recall path"

If your message is long or contains special characters, use the file form instead:

    gt commit -F /tmp/commit-msg.txt

There is no other form. Raw git commit -m is not an alternative — it bypasses the gt toolchain entirely. The calling agent composes the message in its own LLM turn by reading the staged diff; it does not rely on any external API or separate binary. The gt binary is self-contained.

One optional enhancement worth knowing about: if you want pre-commit secret scanning active, enable it once with gt configure --redact-secrets=on. After that, every gt commit call will scan the staged diff for AWS keys, GitHub tokens, JWTs, and similar patterns and abort the commit if any match. It is off by default, so a plain gt commit "message" proceeds without scanning unless you have explicitly turned it on.

Bottom line: stage your changes, read git diff --cached, compose the message, run gt commit "message". That is the full path.

Here is the step-by-step process an agent follows to compose and write a commit message in the Blackrim framework.

Step 1: Read the staged diff.

    git diff --cached

The agent must read this output in its own context window before writing anything. The diff is the source of truth for the message — not the task description, not memory, not what the agent thinks it changed.

Step 2: Compose the message in the agent's LLM turn.

The agent analyzes the diff and writes a commit message that focuses on the why rather than the what. A message like "fix race condition in scorer registration" tells future readers why the change matters. A message like "edit internal/scorer/registry.go" does not. The message is a product of the agent's own reasoning over the diff — no external API call is required.

Step 3: Pass the message to gt commit.

    gt commit "<composed message>"

For messages that exceed a single line or contain characters that would be mangled by shell quoting, use the file form:

    gt commit -F /tmp/commit-msg.txt

Step 4: If gt commit fails, fix the underlying issue.

There is no fallback path. Raw git commit -m is not used. If gt commit fails — pre-commit hook error, secret scan abort, malformed message — the agent investigates and resolves the root cause, then retries gt commit. It does not bypass to a raw git command.

Agents with Bash access (Builder, Yatima, Paolo, Karpal, Renata, Blanca) commit and push from inside their worktree and report the branch name and commit SHA in their final message to the calling citizen. Agents without Bash (Architect, Writer, Researcher) leave files uncommitted and Gestalt handles the commit after reviewing the content.

No. There is no situation where falling back to raw git commit -m is correct in the Blackrim framework. The CLAUDE.md is unambiguous on this point:

"There is no fallback path. Raw git commit -m is not used; if gt commit fails, fix the underlying issue rather than bypassing."

This is not a soft preference — it is the rule. The reason the rule exists is that gt commit is the integration point for the toolchain: it is where pre-commit secret scanning runs (when enabled via gt configure --redact-secrets=on), where commit-message validation hooks fire, and where the audit trail for agent-authored commits is consistent. Bypassing it with a raw git commit -m silently skips all of that.

When gt commit fails, the correct response is to diagnose and fix the failure, then retry gt commit. Common failure modes and their fixes:

- Pre-commit hook error: read the hook output, fix whatever the hook caught (lint failure, test failure, missing factory frontmatter), re-stage, and retry.
- Secret scan abort (when redact-secrets is on): remove or redact the matched pattern from the staged changes, re-stage, and retry.
- Malformed message: fix the quoting or switch to the file form (gt commit -F /tmp/msg.txt) and retry.

If you find yourself reasoning toward "I'll just use git commit -m this once because gt commit is being difficult," that is the anti-rationalization. Do not ship the assumption that gt commit is optional. Fix the failure. The commit path exists specifically so there is one place to enforce quality gates — bypassing it defeats the whole point.

Enable pre-commit secret scanning with a single configuration command:

    gt configure --redact-secrets=on

After running that once, every subsequent gt commit call automatically scans the staged diff for secret patterns — AWS access keys, GitHub tokens, JWTs, and similar credentials — before the commit lands. If any pattern matches, gt commit aborts with an error and the commit does not proceed. You then remove or redact the matched content from your staged changes and retry gt commit.

The scanning is off by default. You do not need to install a separate tool, configure a git hook manually, or change anything in your repository. The capability is built into the gt binary and activates through the single gt configure flag.

The full commit workflow with scanning enabled looks like this:

    git diff --cached           # read the staged diff, compose your message
    gt commit "your message"   # gt scans staged diff, commits only if clean

If the scan catches something:

    # fix or redact the secret in the relevant file
    git add <file>
    gt commit "your message"   # retry after the fix

If you want to check whether redact-secrets is currently on or off, inspect your gt configuration. The setting persists across sessions once set.

One thing to keep in mind: the no-fallback rule still applies here. If gt commit aborts due to a secret scan hit, you fix the staged content and retry gt commit. You do not fall back to git commit -m to sidestep the scan. The scan is the point — circumventing it defeats the protection the feature provides.

Use bin/blackrim-merge-agent to integrate the agent branch back into main. Do not cherry-pick and do not merge manually — the script handles the full workflow cleanly.

The standard invocation is:

    bin/blackrim-merge-agent worktree-agent-<id>

That runs four steps in order: creates a PR via gh pr create pointing from the agent branch to main, squash-merges it with gh pr merge --squash --delete-branch (which auto-closes the PR and auto-deletes the remote branch), fast-forwards your local main with git fetch origin main && git checkout main && git pull --rebase, and prunes the local worktree reference with git worktree prune.

If the repo has branch protection enabled and gh pr merge refuses with a policy error, re-run with the --admin flag:

    bin/blackrim-merge-agent worktree-agent-<id> --admin

This requires admin rights on the repo. In Blackrim's setup, the real quality gate is the local build + test run the agent already performed, not GitHub's required-checks enforcement, so --admin is the normal path on protected-main repos.

If gh authentication is unavailable (offline, no token), fall back to --local:

    bin/blackrim-merge-agent worktree-agent-<id> --local

The --local mode rebases the branch onto main, fast-forward-merges locally, pushes, and deletes the branch — no PR created.

After any merge, verify the worktree is clean: git worktree list should no longer show the agent worktree, and git branch -r | grep worktree-agent should come back empty for that agent's branch. If stale worktrees accumulate from interrupted runs, git worktree prune clears them.

No, cherry-pick is the wrong approach in Blackrim and the CLAUDE.md explicitly prohibits it for agent branch integration. Use bin/blackrim-merge-agent instead.

The core problem with cherry-pick: it creates a new SHA on main, but the agent's branch on origin still points at the original commit with the old SHA. GitHub has no way to detect that the content landed — it sees the branch as unmerged because no merge event was recorded against that PR. At scale this produces stranded branches and open PRs that are actually closed-but-don't-know-it. With five or more agents running concurrently, that accumulates fast.

bin/blackrim-merge-agent avoids this entirely. The standard invocation:

    bin/blackrim-merge-agent worktree-agent-<id>

It goes through gh pr create and gh pr merge --squash --delete-branch, which records the merge relationship on GitHub, auto-closes the PR, and auto-deletes the remote branch in a single atomic operation. The agent branch is gone from origin as soon as the merge completes, and GitHub's merge history correctly shows the content landed.

If branch protection blocks the merge, --admin bypasses policy (requires repo admin). If gh is unavailable, --local does a local rebase-and-fast-forward with a direct push — still not cherry-pick.

The one legitimate use of git cherry-pick against an agent branch is for auditing or testing an individual commit in isolation — not for landing work. If that's what your teammate meant, that's fine. But as the standard integration path, cherry-pick will strand branches and the CLAUDE.md is unambiguous: never use it for agent branch merges.

Using git cherry-pick to bring an agent branch's commit onto main produces a specific failure mode that is hard to notice until branches accumulate: the agent's branch on origin becomes stranded.

Here is what happens step by step. Cherry-pick creates a new commit object on main with the same diff but a different SHA. The agent's original branch on origin still points at the old commit — the one with the original SHA. From GitHub's perspective, that branch has never been merged, because there is no merge base relationship between the new cherry-picked commit on main and the branch tip. GitHub will continue showing the branch as open and unmerged.

If a PR was created for the agent branch, it will also remain open. GitHub cannot auto-close it because the merge that closed it used a different commit object. At scale — five agents running concurrently through a full bd queue — this creates a graveyard of stranded branches and zombie PRs. The cleanup is manual and tedious.

The worktree local state has a parallel problem: git worktree prune cannot clean up the local worktree entry if the branch is still showing as active on origin.

The correct workflow is bin/blackrim-merge-agent, which goes through gh pr merge --squash --delete-branch. That call records the merge relationship on GitHub, auto-closes the associated PR, and auto-deletes the remote branch atomically. Main gets a single squash commit, the branch is gone, and the PR is closed — no stranding.

If you have already cherry-picked and the branch is now stranded, you can clean it up manually: delete the remote branch with git push origin --delete worktree-agent-<id>, close the PR explicitly via gh pr close, and run git worktree prune locally. Then resolve to use bin/blackrim-merge-agent for all future agent branch integrations.

Pass the --local flag:

    bin/blackrim-merge-agent worktree-agent-<id> --local

The --local mode skips the PR workflow entirely. It rebases the agent branch onto main, fast-forward-merges locally, pushes the updated main to origin, and deletes the remote branch — all without creating a GitHub PR. This is the correct choice when gh authentication is unavailable, you are working offline, or the repo setup does not support the PR workflow.

The full default flow (no flag) is: gh pr create, then gh pr merge --squash --delete-branch, then git fetch + checkout + pull --rebase main locally, then git worktree prune. That's the preferred path when gh is available because GitHub records the merge relationship and auto-closes the PR.

The --admin flag is a different use case: it bypasses GitHub branch protection policies when the default gh pr merge is refused with a policy error. It still goes through the PR path, it just passes admin privileges to override the required-checks gate. Use --admin when the repo has branch protection and you have admin rights; use --local when the PR infrastructure is not available at all.

After --local completes, verify: git log --oneline origin/main should show the squashed commit, and git branch -r | grep worktree-agent-<id> should return empty. If the local worktree reference is still listed by git worktree list, run git worktree prune to clear it.

One thing --local does not give you: the GitHub merge event and PR audit trail. If that history matters for your workflow (compliance, review records), prefer the default or --admin path and only fall back to --local when you have no other option.
